narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pipelines, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 51% chance of failure—meaning it leaned toward expecting you might not pass initially, which led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't driven by your background alone but by a careful look at key academic indicators compared to thousands of past students. The most influential factor pulling against you was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to peers who ranked higher. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive pull, pushing the prediction toward success because it's stronger than most applicants'. Your lower-middle family income also helped, nudging things favorably as the model sees resilience in students from similar backgrounds often succeeding. Less impactful but still noted were your Year 1 decile of 2 (below average, adding some concern) and your LSAT score of 28 (lower than the typical 36.7, contributing mildly to the risk). Being a full-time student was a small plus, aligning with the vast majority who pursue it that way. Overall, your profile sits below typical successful applicants in key decile and test score areas, even with your GPA edge, tipping the scales to failure odds just over 50%. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts with targeted prep. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking mentorship to improve class rankings, or exploring programs with bar prep support tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward.
